findings.title: Track-A weights sum to 0.95, not 1.0 (5% silent score loss)
findings.description: scoreTrackA defines weights = { hyper: 0.30, rule: 0.25, scaling: 0.20, af: 0.20 } (=0.95) when AF applicable, and { hyper: 0.38, rule: 0.30, scaling: 0.27, af: 0 } (=0.95) when AF missing. Neither set sums to 1.0.
findings.mechanism: coreScore = sum(weight_i * subscore_i). With weights summing to 0.95, even a perfect 100-everywhere stock can only achieve coreScore=95 before penalties. Worse: AF-applicable path appears to weight 5pp differently than AF-missing path so cross-stock comparability is broken (a stock with AF=80 vs identical stock without AF gets slightly different mathematical treatment beyond just the AF contribution).
findings.reproducer: Run scoreTrackA on a synthetic stock where hyper=100, ruleC=100, scaling=100, af=100 (applicable). coreScore = 0.30*100 + 0.25*100 + 0.20*100 + 0.20*100 = 95, not 100.
findings.impact: Final scores are systematically depressed ~5%. The A-bucket threshold (75) is harder to hit than intended. Bucket distribution skews toward B/INFLECTION. Penalties have outsized relative impact.
findings.suggested_fix: Rebalance weights to sum exactly to 1.0. E.g. { hyper: 0.32, rule: 0.26, scaling: 0.21, af: 0.21 } and { hyper: 0.40, rule: 0.32, scaling: 0.28, af: 0 }. Or document explicitly that the 5% gap is reserved headroom.

findings.title: Track-B weights sum to 0.95, not 1.0 (same bug as Track A)
findings.description: scoreTrackB defines weights = { roic: 0.22, gm: 0.20, fcf: 0.18, cagr: 0.15, af: 0.20 } (=0.95) when AF applicable, and { roic: 0.28, gm: 0.25, fcf: 0.22, cagr: 0.20, af: 0 } (=0.95) when AF missing. Neither sums to 1.0.
findings.mechanism: Same as Track A: maximum achievable coreScore is 95 instead of 100. Penalties (which subtract raw points) become disproportionate.
findings.reproducer: Run scoreTrackB on a synthetic stock with all sub-scores = 100. coreScore = 95.
findings.impact: Track-B final scores systematically depressed. A/B-bucket thresholds (75/60) are effectively raised by ~5pp.
findings.suggested_fix: Rebalance to sum=1.0. E.g. { roic: 0.23, gm: 0.21, fcf: 0.19, cagr: 0.16, af: 0.21 } and { roic: 0.30, gm: 0.26, fcf: 0.23, cagr: 0.21, af: 0 }.

findings.title: Track-B INFLECTION bucket has no mcap-downgrade rule (asymmetric vs Track A)
findings.description: Track A downgrades buckets A→B/INFLECTION, B→INFLECTION/SPEC, and INFLECTION→SPEC when growth is below thresholds. Track B downgrades A→B/INFLECTION, B→INFLECTION/SPEC by market cap, but does NOT downgrade INFLECTION→SPEC for tiny-cap stocks. A $3B-mcap stock that scores 55 in Track B stays INFLECTION; the comparable Track-A logic would push it to SPEC.
findings.mechanism: Missing else-branch for bucket.id === 'INFLECTION' && mcap < small-cap threshold. Track B is asymmetric in its bucket-correction rules.
findings.reproducer: Stock with mcap $2B, ROIC=70, GM=70, FCF=70, CAGR=50 → coreScore ≈ 0.95*60 = 57 → bucket=INFLECTION → stays INFLECTION even though stock fails Track-B universe ($50B floor).
findings.impact: Sub-universe stocks slip through Track B without bucket correction. Inconsistent with Track A. Downstream consumers see INFLECTION/REVIEW status for stocks that shouldn't qualify for Track B at all.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: `else if (bucket.id === 'INFLECTION' && mcap < 10e9) bucket = BUCKETS[3];` in Track B.

findings.title: Cross-profile detection requires Track-B universe (mcap >=$50B) — small-cap dual-quality stocks miss flag
findings.description: isCrossProfile / MULTI_PROFILE_EXPOSURE requires BOTH passesTrackAUniverse AND passesTrackBUniverse. passesTrackBUniverse hard-requires mcap >= 50e9 (line 1146). A $10B hyper-growth compounder with FCF+OpM positive and 40% growth can NEVER be flagged cross-profile.
findings.mechanism: Track-B universe gate is too strict for the cross-profile concept. The intent of MULTI_PROFILE_EXPOSURE is to highlight stocks that pass both growth and quality screens, but the $50B floor was meant for the Track-B *score* not the *quality check*.
findings.reproducer: Stock with mcap=$15B, growth=60%, FCF=15%, OpM=10% → passesTrackA=true, passesTrackB=false (mcap<50B) → isCrossProfile=false even though it's clearly dual-track-eligible.
findings.impact: Mid-cap dual-quality stocks (CRDO, ALAB, etc.) lose the MULTI_PROFILE_EXPOSURE reasonCode. Dashboard users miss cross-profile signals on growing compounders below $50B.
findings.suggested_fix: Decouple universe-pass logic from cross-profile detection. Add separate `meetsTrackBQuality(stock)` that checks FCF/OpM/growth without the $50B mcap floor; use that in isCrossProfile.

findings.title: Coverage hard-requires meta.sector|industry — ticker-override stocks still get Coverage=0
findings.description: computeCoverage returns 0 if `!hasSector` (meta.sector || meta.industry missing). But classifySubProfile checks TICKER_SUBPROFILE_MAP BEFORE sector/industry. A stock with valid ticker (e.g., NVDA) but missing/blank sector from Yahoo would: (a) classify correctly as HARDWARE via ticker override, (b) get Coverage=0 → UNCLASSIFIABLE_DATA_RISK.
findings.mechanism: Two classifications systems (ticker map vs sector-keyword) disagree about what constitutes 'enough metadata'. Coverage uses the strict version.
findings.reproducer: Build stock with meta = { ticker: 'NVDA', sector: null, industry: null, fetchedAt: 'today' }, marketCap = 2.8T, all metrics filled. classifySubProfile returns HARDWARE (correct). computeCoverage returns 0. scoreTrackA returns UNCLASSIFIABLE_DATA_RISK with COVERAGE_INSUFFICIENT — despite full data.
findings.impact: Stocks where Yahoo drops sector metadata get marked UNCLASSIFIABLE even when ticker-override gave us a confident classification. Symptom: occasional flapping UNCLASSIFIABLE for known stocks.
findings.suggested_fix: In computeCoverage, treat ticker-override hit as equivalent to hasSector. Or: relax hasSector to include ticker presence when ticker is in TICKER_SUBPROFILE_MAP.

findings.title: HARD_PENALTY_CODES set contains codes never emitted by engine (dead detection)
findings.description: score-orchestrator declares HARD_PENALTY_CODES = { EXCLUDE_CASH_RUNWAY, EXCLUDE_MCAP_LOW, EXCLUDE_DILUTION_EXTREME, SBC_EXTREME_HARD, CORPORATE_ACTION_RISK }. Engine emits EXCLUDE_CASH_RUNWAY, EXCLUDE_MCAP_LOW, SBC_EXTREME_HARD. Neither EXCLUDE_DILUTION_EXTREME nor CORPORATE_ACTION_RISK is ever emitted anywhere in engine-v7.3.js or manipulation-filters.js.
findings.mechanism: Schema drift between consumer (orchestrator) and producer (engine). Comments/docs reference EXCLUDE_DILUTION_EXTREME but no code path generates it. CORPORATE_ACTION_RISK is also fully orphaned.
findings.reproducer: Grep for 'EXCLUDE_DILUTION_EXTREME' and 'CORPORATE_ACTION_RISK' across all *.js — only score-orchestrator.js references them.
findings.impact: Dead defensive code in orchestrator. Risk: if engine adds a real dilution check later and emits 'EXCLUDE_DILUTION_HIGH' (not EXTREME), it won't be flagged as hard penalty unless the isHardPenalty fallback heuristic (EXCLUDE_* prefix) catches it. The static list creates false confidence.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) remove unused codes from HARD_PENALTY_CODES, or (b) implement the producer side in engine. Or (c) document that this is forward-compat for upcoming features.

findings.title: Marketplace manipulation filter code/doc/comment mismatch on threshold logic
findings.description: Filter docstring (lines 139-141) says: 'Wenn Revenue-Growth ≥30% UND OpInc-Growth < (Rev-Growth - 10pp)'. Actual implementation (line 159) checks `marginDelta < -5`, i.e., OpInc-margin drop > 5pp. These are mathematically different. Doc says comparing two growth rates; code compares two margins.
findings.mechanism: The implementation was changed (per the inline comment 'OpInc kann negativ sein. Pragmatisch: pp-Differenz auf Margin-Basis statt Growth-Rate.') but the headline docstring/contract was not updated.
findings.reproducer: Stock with Rev-Growth=35% and OpInc-Growth=20%. Doc-implied: 35-20=15pp > 10pp → fire. Actual code: comparing margin delta which depends on absolute margin values, not growth rates → may not fire.
findings.impact: Reviewer confusion when debugging false-negatives. The filter fires on different inputs than the documentation suggests. Score consumers expecting old behavior get unexpected flagging patterns.
findings.suggested_fix: Update docstring to describe margin-delta logic. Or revert to growth-rate logic if that was intended.

findings.title: Industrial earnings-quality filter negative-OpInc branch uses asymmetric threshold (50% vs 150%)
findings.description: Positive-OpInc branch: triggers when `ni > oi * 1.5` (150% of OpInc). Negative-OpInc branch: triggers when `ni > Math.abs(oi) * 0.5 && ni > 0` (50% of |OpInc|). For a company with OpInc=-100M and NetIncome=+60M, the negative branch fires (60 > 50). But the same company in mirror — OpInc=+100M, NetIncome=+60M — does NOT fire (60 < 150).
findings.mechanism: The negative-branch threshold (0.5*|oi|) is much looser than the positive-branch (1.5*oi). Inline doc says 'one-time gains > -OpInc — also mehr als den ganzen operativen Verlust kompensiert' but the implemented check only requires gains > 50% of |OpInc|.
findings.reproducer: Stock A: oi=[100, 100, 100], ni=[60, 60, 60] — no flag. Stock B: oi=[-100, -100, -100], ni=[60, 60, 60] — EARNINGS_QUALITY_LOW_INDUSTRIAL fires in 3/3 years.
findings.impact: Industrial companies emerging from operational losses get flagged for low earnings quality far more readily than profitable ones — likely the inverse of the intended signal. Companies with one-time gains exactly equal to OpInc loss (full offset) would fire, but the docstring implies the threshold should be 100% offset.
findings.suggested_fix: Use consistent threshold semantics. E.g. negative branch: `ni > Math.abs(oi)` (gains > full loss offset).

findings.title: ManipulationFilters has no filters for BANK / REIT / CONSUMER_STAPLES / ENERGY / FINTECH / HEALTHCARE
findings.description: FILTERS_BY_PROFILE only registers filter functions for SAAS/HARDWARE/INDUSTRIAL/MARKETPLACE. BANK, REIT, CONSUMER_STAPLES, ENERGY are not listed at all (so evaluate returns skipped:'no_profile_match'). FINTECH, HEALTHCARE, OTHER are listed with empty arrays (returns empty codes).
findings.mechanism: Filter registry incomplete vs SUB_PROFILES taxonomy. BANK/REIT/CONSUMER_STAPLES/ENERGY were added later (Tag-26) without filter coverage.
findings.reproducer: Stock classified as BANK or REIT. evaluate() returns { skipped: 'no_profile_match' } not { codes: [] }. UI/consumer must handle two different no-match shapes.
findings.impact: Inconsistent return shape. Bank/insurer/REIT stocks get zero anti-manipulation coverage, but the empty-codes case for FINTECH/HEALTHCARE/OTHER provides the same effective coverage with a different return shape. Confusing for consumers.
findings.suggested_fix: Add the missing profile keys with empty arrays to FILTERS_BY_PROFILE so the return shape is consistent.

findings.title: Bucket downgrade uses hardcoded BUCKETS array indices — fragile to reorder
findings.description: Track A and B downgrade logic uses BUCKETS[1], BUCKETS[2], BUCKETS[3] as literals. If BUCKETS array order ever changes, downgrades silently map to wrong buckets.
findings.mechanism: Tight coupling between array order and semantic intent. No tests guard against reorder.
findings.reproducer: Reorder BUCKETS to put SPEC before INFLECTION → all downgrades route to wrong bucket → A→B logic moves stocks to SPEC instead of INFLECTION.
findings.impact: Latent maintenance bug. Will surface as silent score corruption the day someone reorders BUCKETS for ordering convenience.
findings.suggested_fix: Define lookup helper: `bucketById(id) { return BUCKETS.find(b => b.id === id); }` and use bucketById('B'), bucketById('INFLECTION'), bucketById('SPEC').

findings.title: scoreSnapshot sorts UNCLASSIFIABLE results with finalScore=null as score=0
findings.description: After scoreTrackA returns UNCLASSIFIABLE_DATA_RISK (finalScore=null) and scoreTrackB returns a valid score=45, the sort `(b.finalScore || 0) - (a.finalScore || 0)` treats null as 0. Track B (45) wins over Track A (null→0). That's fine on its face — but if BOTH tracks return UNCLASSIFIABLE (e.g., stale data), candidates[0] is the first-sorted UNCLASSIFIABLE result and downstream code still proceeds to append manipulation-filter codes / orchestratorVersion to a null-finalScore object.
findings.mechanism: scoreSnapshot doesn't distinguish 'no track applicable' from 'tracks ran but data insufficient'. The `if (!score)` check at line 222 is unreachable when candidates.length > 0 because UNCLASSIFIABLE results are still truthy objects.
findings.reproducer: Stock with isStale=true (no filingDate) → both Track A and Track B return finalScore=null, actionStatus=UNCLASSIFIABLE_DATA_RISK. scoreSnapshot returns one of them with manipulation-filter codes possibly appended (the codes ManipulationFilters.evaluate emits even for UNCLASSIFIABLE inputs).
findings.impact: Downstream code may see actionStatus=UNCLASSIFIABLE_DATA_RISK alongside manipulation-filter codes, which is logically inconsistent (no score → no anti-manipulation context). Minor confusion; may flap reasonCodes between runs based on Yahoo timing.
findings.suggested_fix: Skip manipulation-filter evaluation when finalScore is null or actionStatus is UNCLASSIFIABLE_DATA_RISK.

findings.title: Engine-fixtures: MNDY fixture expects DISQUALIFIED but inline comment says intent is QUALIFIED
findings.description: MNDY fixture has `expected: { ..., actionStatus: 'DISQUALIFIED' }` but the preceding comment explicitly states 'ChatGPT-P0-Fix-6: MNDY nicht blind DISQUALIFIED. SBC=28% + neg OpMargin sind WARNINGS (PROFITABILITY_NOT_ESTABLISHED, SBC_EXTREME), nicht Hard-Exclusion. Bei pre-profit-Inflection-SaaS muss Track A das zulassen.'
findings.mechanism: Expected-value contradicts the documented design intent. Either the fix was reverted but comment kept, or expected wasn't updated.
findings.reproducer: Open engine-fixtures.js line 435 → expected.actionStatus = 'DISQUALIFIED'. Read lines 432-434 → comment claims expectation should be QUALIFIED with warnings.
findings.impact: Test fixture validates the wrong contract. If the engine ever correctly emits QUALIFIED for MNDY (per Fix-6 intent), the test fails; if it emits DISQUALIFIED, the documented Fix-6 is silently regressed.
findings.suggested_fix: Reconcile expected with intent. Per the comment, change expected to { bucketAtLeast: 'INFLECTION', actionStatus: 'QUALIFIED' } OR remove the contradictory comment if DISQUALIFIED is genuinely desired.

findings.title: computeScalingEfficiency falls through for efficiency < -20 (score stays at 0, no else clause)
findings.description: Score-mapping ladder: efficiency >=30 → 100, >=15 → 80+, >=5 → 50+, >=-5 → 25+, >=-20 → 10+. No else branch for efficiency < -20, so score remains at initial 0. The asymmetric floor at line 458 may bump it to 40, but only when growth>70 AND GM>=50. For everyone else, score=0.
findings.mechanism: Missing terminal else clause. Acceptable behavior (very bad efficiency → 0 score), but is implicit rather than explicit.
findings.reproducer: Stock with efficiency = -30 (e.g., OpEx grew 40% YoY, GP grew 10%) → score=0 (or 40 if growth>70 AND GM>=50).
findings.impact: Hard 0-cliff at efficiency=-20. A stock at -19.9 gets ~10, at -20.0 gets 0. Step discontinuity.
findings.suggested_fix: Add explicit `else score = 0;` or extend the ladder smoothly (e.g., `else score = Math.max(0, 10 + (efficiency + 20))`).

findings.title: computeRuleOfX score=5 default applies even when applicable=false
findings.description: Function computes 'score' for rox regardless of `applicable`, then returns `score: applicable ? score : 0`. But the captured `rox` object on the return has `score: applicable ? score : 0` while keeping the raw rox value. computeRuleComposite uses `Math.max(rox.score, ro40.score)` so when SaaS-applicable returns clamped score and non-applicable returns 0 — composite then equals ro40.score. Functionally OK, but: when applicable=false, the rox value (e.g., 250) is still exposed in result.rox.rox even though rox.score=0. UI may display 'Rule-of-X: 250' next to 'score=0' which is confusing.
findings.mechanism: Internal/external semantics of 'applicable' don't propagate cleanly. Raw rox is always computed.
findings.reproducer: Stock = INDUSTRIAL, growth=80%, fcfM=20 → isSaaSLike=false → applicable=false → rox raw = 220, score=0. Returned object: { score: 0, rox: 220, applicable: false }. Consumer reading rox.rox sees 220.
findings.impact: Cosmetic + reviewer confusion. Not score-breaking but adds noise in dev/audit output.
findings.suggested_fix: When !applicable, set rox to null or skip computation.

findings.title: Acceleration filter docstring mentions OPEX_GROWTH_LAGS but code emits margin-delta
findings.description: The OPINC_LAGS_REVENUE_MARKETPLACE filter docs say it fires when 'Revenue-Growth ≥30% UND OpInc-Growth < (Rev-Growth - 10pp)' but the code only enters branch if `revGrowth >= 30` then checks `marginDelta < -5`. A stock with revGrowth=29% and OpInc-margin dropping 20pp will NOT fire — design intent unclear whether the 30% gate should be relaxed.
findings.mechanism: Pre-condition `revGrowth < 30` skips out entirely (`return null`) before checking the margin delta. So the filter ONLY fires for hyper-growth + margin compression. Stocks that decelerated below 30% growth with margin collapse escape the filter.
findings.reproducer: Stock with revGrowth=25%, OpInc-margin from 10% → -10% (20pp drop) → returns null at line 150.
findings.impact: Marketplace stocks decelerating from hyper-growth into stagnation with margin compression don't get flagged. The filter is narrowly targeted at hyper-growth scaling failures, not at general profitability deterioration.
findings.suggested_fix: Document this explicitly. Or split into two filters: one for hyper-growth scaling, one for any-growth margin collapse.

findings.title: computeRevenueAcceleration uses fcfMarginTTM as 'fcfDelta' — variable misnamed
findings.description: In SAAS branch: `const fcfDelta = (getMetricValue(stock, 'fcfMarginTTM') || 0); if (acceleration >= 10 && fcfDelta < 10)`. The variable name 'fcfDelta' suggests delta-FCF (change), but it's actually absolute fcfMarginTTM%. Logic uses 'FCF margin below 10%' as a deferred-revenue proxy.
findings.mechanism: Variable naming bug. Logic is intended (SaaS with low FCF margin during acceleration is suspect), but misleading name suggests the check is wrong.
findings.reproducer: Read code at line 528-530 — `fcfDelta` is a fresh read of fcfMarginTTM, not a delta.
findings.impact: Maintainability hazard. Future developer may 'fix' this thinking it's a bug and switch to actual delta-FCF — changing semantics.
findings.suggested_fix: Rename to `fcfMarginAbs` or `fcfM`. Add inline comment explaining the absolute-margin threshold logic.

findings.title: passesTrackAUniverse uses `(g || 0)` — null growth silently fails universe check
findings.description: passesTrackAUniverse: `return mcapUSD != null && mcapUSD >= 2e9 && (g || 0) >= 40;`. When revenueGrowthYoY is null/undefined, `(null || 0) = 0`, fails the >= 40 check, so universe-not-passing. Same `(g || 0) < 40` is used in scoreTrackA reasonCode logic (line 975-976).
findings.mechanism: Coalescing null to 0 silently masks the data-quality issue. A stock with no growth data is treated identically to a stock with confirmed 0% growth.
findings.reproducer: Stock with revenueGrowthYoY = null (Yahoo did not return field). passesTrackAUniverse returns false. UNIVERSE_TRACK_A_FAILED appears in reasonCodes — caller cannot distinguish 'low growth' from 'no growth data'.
findings.impact: Data-quality issue masked. Orchestrator's _fillDerivedMetrics tries to fill the gap from annual, but if annual is also missing, the stock falls through silently. Distinguishing 'unknown' from 'zero' matters for confidence scoring.
findings.suggested_fix: Use explicit null check: `g != null && g >= 40`. Emit separate reasonCode UNIVERSE_TRACK_A_NO_GROWTH_DATA when g is null.

findings.title: isStaleData treats invalid date strings as stale rather than as data error
findings.description: isStaleData: when filingDate is unparseable (e.g., 'invalid string'), `new Date(...).getTime()` returns NaN, `Number.isFinite(NaN)` is false → returns true (stale). The caller treats stale-data as UNCLASSIFIABLE_DATA_RISK with reason OLD_DATA_STALE — but the actual issue is corrupted metadata, not old data.
findings.mechanism: Conflates 'stale' (old) with 'broken' (unparseable). Defensive behavior is correct (don't trust the data) but the reason code is misleading.
findings.reproducer: Stock with meta.filingDate = '2026-13-45' (invalid month) → isStaleData returns true → reasonCode OLD_DATA_STALE.
findings.impact: Diagnostic noise. Users see OLD_DATA_STALE on stocks with broken meta, then check filing dates and see they're current. Real issue is meta corruption.
findings.suggested_fix: Return distinct reason DATE_PARSING_FAILED when input is unparseable. Or: keep returning true but log a warning when t is non-finite.

findings.title: Track-B action status: bucket=OUT check is unreachable when hardExcluded=true forces bucket=OUT
findings.description: Track-B's action status chain: `if (hardExcluded) DISQUALIFIED; else if (A||B) QUALIFIED; else if (OUT) DISQUALIFIED; else REVIEW`. But bucketFor() returns BUCKETS[4] (OUT) ONLY when score==null OR hardExcluded=true. So bucket=OUT is achievable when hardExcluded=false only if score IS null in the bucketFor path. In scoreTrackB, finalScore is `Math.max(0, Math.min(100, coreScore + pen.total))` — always finite. The score==null branch in bucketFor is dead. So `bucket.id === 'OUT' && !hardExcluded` requires finalScore=0 (score>=b.min=0 matches OUT before iterating). Reachable in theory but the linear iteration: `for (b of BUCKETS) if (score >= b.min) return b;` — A first (min 75), if score < 75 continues; B (min 60); INFLECTION (min 50); SPEC (min 40); OUT (min 0). A score of exactly 39 falls through past SPEC into OUT — yes, reachable.
findings.mechanism: Bucket=OUT (no hard penalty) occurs when finalScore < 40 (below SPEC threshold). Both Track A and Track B chains handle this, but Track A folds it into the first `if` while Track B has it as a separate `else if` — inconsistent style. The chain still produces DISQUALIFIED in both cases, so the bug is style, not behavior.
findings.reproducer: Stock with finalScore=35 (low ROIC/GM/FCF/CAGR all weak) and no hard penalties → bucket.id='OUT' → Track B returns DISQUALIFIED via the `else if (bucket.id === 'OUT')` branch.
findings.impact: Style inconsistency between Track A and Track B. No functional bug. But anyone editing the chain may miss the asymmetry.
findings.suggested_fix: Make both tracks use the same form: `if (hardExcluded || bucket.id === 'OUT') DISQUALIFIED; else if (A||B) QUALIFIED; else REVIEW;`.

findings.title: computePenalties: revenue decel branch when lastG >= 50 has unreachable but absent reasonCode
findings.description: Decel block: when lastG >= 50 → 'Still hyper — no penalty' (no codes). When lastG >= 30 → checks decel > 30. When lastG >= 15 → checks decel > 15. Else → various decels. But for lastG = 50 with prevG = 200 (massive decel from 200% to 50%), no PENALTY_GROWTH_DECEL is emitted. A stock decelerating from hyper-hyper (200%) to merely-hyper (50%) is one of the most common red flags in growth stocks (e.g., post-pandemic Zoom).
findings.mechanism: The 'still hyper' branch is too lenient. Hyper-to-merely-hyper deceleration is materially different from merely-hyper-staying-hyper.
findings.reproducer: Stock with annualRev = [r0=150, r1=100, r2=33] → lastG = 50%, prevG = 203% → decel = 153pp. No penalty fires.
findings.impact: Hyper-decelerating stocks (Zoom-circa-2022, post-vaccine biotech) score better than they should. Karl/users miss critical mean-reversion signal.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: `if (lastG >= 50) { if (decel > 50) { penalties.push({...-10}); codes.push('PENALTY_HYPER_DECEL'); } }`.

findings.title: Sub-profile classification: 'OTHER' ticker symbol literally matches as ticker override
findings.description: Edge case: if a stock has meta.ticker = 'OTHER' (literally), the check `TICKER_SUBPROFILE_MAP[ticker]` would lookup 'OTHER' which is undefined (the SUB_PROFILES has OTHER, but TICKER_SUBPROFILE_MAP doesn't). So falls through to sector check. Safe. But more subtle: if a future ticker map entry uses the string 'OTHER' as a value, classification would route to SUB_PROFILES.OTHER, masking intent.
findings.mechanism: String collision potential between sub-profile IDs and ticker strings. Currently safe — no real stock has ticker 'OTHER'. Defensive coding for future expansion.
findings.reproducer: Add 'BANK' as a stock ticker (hypothetical: a 'Bank of XYZ' with ticker BANK). The classification flow would not match BANK in TICKER_SUBPROFILE_MAP, fall through to sector matching, which works correctly. Not a real bug.
findings.impact: Latent risk only if TICKER_SUBPROFILE_MAP is ever expanded carelessly.
findings.suggested_fix: Optional: validate ticker map keys against known real tickers (e.g., have a known-tickers set in a separate file).

findings.title: computeFCFQuality fallback path returns score without populating positiveYears/totalYears
findings.description: When `fcf.length < 2`, function returns `{ score: s, fallback: true }` without `positiveYears`, `totalYears`, or `ratio`. Consumers reading these fields (e.g., dashboard table) get undefined. Inconsistent return shape vs the main path which returns full fields.
findings.mechanism: Two return shapes for one function. Caller must defensively check `fallback: true`.
findings.reproducer: Stock with annualFCF.length = 1 (one year only) → returns { score, fallback: true } — no other fields.
findings.impact: Dashboard / UI columns showing 'FCF positive years' display undefined/NaN/'N/A' inconsistently depending on which return path fired.
findings.suggested_fix: Always return same shape, e.g., `{ score, positiveYears: null, totalYears: validFCF.length, ratio: null, fallback: true }`.

findings.title: _filter_industrial_earnings_quality oldest-vs-newest order ambiguity
findings.description: Filter uses `.slice(0, 3)` on annualOpInc/annualNetIncome. If Yahoo convention is newest-first (per engine-v7.3.js comments at line 704: 'Yahoo gives annual newest-first. last = newest, oldest = back of array.'), then `.slice(0, 3)` gives the 3 most-recent years. OK. But _arrayValues strips null/non-number entries — if year-old data has a null OpInc, the array is compacted and indices shift, so what looks like 'Y-0' may actually be Y-1 or Y-2.
findings.mechanism: Filtering removes nulls without preserving position. The evidence labels (`Y-${i}`) become misleading.
findings.reproducer: annualOpInc = [{value:100}, {value:null}, {value:80}, {value:70}], annualNetIncome = [{value:160}, {value:120}, {value:130}, {value:110}]. After _arrayValues: oi=[100,80,70], ni=[160,120,130,110]. Slice(0,3): oi=[100,80,70], ni=[160,120,130]. Loop compares oi[1]=80 with ni[1]=120 — but oi[1] is actually Y-2 and ni[1] is Y-1. Cross-year comparison.
findings.impact: Filter may fire on misaligned year-comparisons. Especially likely for newly-public companies with partial data. Triggers false-positive earnings-quality flags.
findings.suggested_fix: Don't strip nulls before pairing. Iterate over indices with both array values still in place; skip pairs where either is null.

findings.title: scoreSnapshot tiebreaker may return wrong track when both finalScores are null
findings.description: When both Track A and Track B return UNCLASSIFIABLE_DATA_RISK (finalScore=null), the sort `(b.finalScore || 0) - (a.finalScore || 0)` produces 0-0=0, so sort is stable. JavaScript Array.sort is stable as of ES2019. But the universe-failed check `_failedUniverse(a) !== _failedUniverse(b)` may flip ordering: if Track A failed universe but Track B didn't (or vice-versa), the non-failing track wins. In a UNCLASSIFIABLE state, neither track has meaningful score, so the universe-pass winner may be the wrong report (e.g., Track A reports UNCLASSIFIABLE due to coverage, Track B reports UNCLASSIFIABLE due to <3y annual — both correct but their primary reason codes differ).
findings.mechanism: Universe-pass is meaningful only when the score is finite. When score is null, sort dimension switches purely on universe-pass, returning whichever track happens to have universe-pass alignment — not necessarily the track with the more informative reason code.
findings.reproducer: Stock with no growth data and only 2y annual. Track A returns UNCLASSIFIABLE (COVERAGE_INSUFFICIENT). Track B returns UNCLASSIFIABLE (TRACK_B_DISABLED_INSUFFICIENT_DATA). passesA=false → Track A `_failedUniverse` true. passesB=false → Track B `_failedUniverse` true. Both failed → stable sort, Track A first. OK in this case. But if passesA=true (high mcap, prior growth data) and Track B fails on annual depth, Track B's reason gets returned as 'primary' which may be less informative.
findings.impact: Edge-case reasonCode prioritization. Users see one of two valid UNCLASSIFIABLE reasons; the choice may seem arbitrary.
findings.suggested_fix: When both finalScores are null, prefer the track with more reason codes, or merge reason codes from both tracks.

findings.title: buyStatus does not check for UNCLASSIFIABLE actionStatus — returns BUY_READY if bucket somehow set
findings.description: buyStatus checks `scoreResult.bucket.id` exists, not actionStatus. If a score-result has UNCLASSIFIABLE_DATA_RISK actionStatus but somehow has bucket (shouldn't happen, but...), it routes through normal logic. The actual scoreTrackA early-return for UNCLASSIFIABLE doesn't set bucket, so practically buyStatus correctly returns UNCLASSIFIABLE. But the check is bucket-shape-only, not actionStatus-aware.
findings.mechanism: Decoupling between actionStatus and bucket. Both are derived from finalScore, but the buy-status mapping reads one field.
findings.reproducer: Manually construct scoreResult = { bucket: { id: 'A' }, actionStatus: 'UNCLASSIFIABLE_DATA_RISK', reasonCodes: [], hardExcluded: false }. buyStatus returns 'BUY_READY' despite the unclassifiable flag.
findings.impact: Defensive; only fires under hand-crafted/corrupted inputs.
findings.suggested_fix: Add early-return: `if (scoreResult.actionStatus === 'UNCLASSIFIABLE_DATA_RISK') return 'UNCLASSIFIABLE';`.

findings.title: _fillDerivedMetrics confidence values 0.5-0.7 are not propagated to dataConfidence calculation
findings.description: Orchestrator backfills metrics with confidence=0.5/0.6/0.7 but engine's computeDataConfidence only counts presence of metrics, not their confidence values. So a stock with all metrics 'derived' from annual gets coverage=0.85+ and HIGH confidence, even though every metric is a low-confidence approximation.
findings.mechanism: Confidence is metadata that's set but never read by the scorer.
findings.reproducer: Stock with Yahoo missing all four TTM metrics, but with annualRev/annualGP/annualOpInc/annualFCF present. Orchestrator fills all four with confidence 0.5-0.7. Engine's computeCoverage gives full credit (presence-based). dataConfidence returns HIGH.
findings.impact: Score quality overstated. A 'derived' stock looks as confident as a fully-Yahoo-fresh stock.
findings.suggested_fix: Engine should weight metric counts by metric.confidence when computing coverage. Or: orchestrator should add a DERIVED_METRICS_PRESENT reasonCode that downgrades confidence.
